coredata.dc:title: Dual lanthanide-probe based on coordination polymer networks for ratiometric detection of glyphosate in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Fei
Wang, Han
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a new type of coordination polymer networks (CPNs), where both of 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) and guanylate monophosphate (GMP) chelate with Eu3+, are firstly synthesized (GMP/Eu/DPA). After mixing with GMP/Tb CPNs, a novel ratiometric dual lanthanide nanoprobe is constructed. Alkaline phosphohydrolase (ALP) specifically induces the cleavage of phosphate ester group in GMP. Therefore, the addition of ALP causes the fluorescence quenching of GMP/Tb, and then the emissions of GMP/Eu/DPA enhance as the result of the formation of Eu/DPA complexes. Glyphosate, as an organophosphorus pesticide, can vehemently inhibit the catalytic activity of ALP, so a ratiometric detection of glyphosate can be achieved with a linear range from 0.015 to 8.45 μg/mL. The present strategy also shows good recoveries for measuring glyphosate in rice, millet, soybean, milk, tap water, and mountain spring water, suggesting a great potential for applications in foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Coordination polymer networks
Dual lanthanide
Ratiometric detecton
Glyphosate
Food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GMP/Eu, Eu/DPA, GMP/Eu/DPA and GMP/Tb.
Sensitive and selective detection of glyphosate
Detection of glyphosate in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) GMP/Eu, (B) Eu/DPA, and (C) GMP/Eu/DPA; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of GMP/Eu (curve a), Eu/DPA (curve b), and GMP/Eu/DPA (curve c), respectively; (E) Fluorescence spectra of GMP/Eu (curve a), Eu/DPA (curve b), and GMP/Eu/DPA (curve c), respectively (Inset: Digital photos of GMP/Eu (a), Eu/DPA (b), and GMP/Eu/DPA (c) under UV light); (F) FT-IR spectra of GMP (curve a), GMP/Eu (curve b), Eu/DPA (curve c) and GMP/Eu/DPA (curve d), respectively.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA system after adding ALP with different concentrations from 0.1 to 10 U/L and (B) from 10 to 300 U/L; (C) the corresponding linear range of ALP from 0.1 to 10 U/L and (D) from 10 to 300 U/L.
Selectivity of dual lanthanide-probe towards ALP. The concentration of ALP is 10 U/L; the concentrations of metal ions and amino acids are 1 mM; other proteins and enzymes are 1000 U/L.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA system after adding glyphosate in the presence of ALP. (B) The corresponding linear range of glyphosate from 0.015 to 8.45 μg/mL. (C) Influence of common coexistence substances on the glyphosate detection. (D) Influence of other pesticides on the glyphosate detection. The concentration of glyphosate is 1.7 μg/mL (equal to 10 μM); the concentrations of metal ions and amino acids are 1 mM; other pesticides are 1 mM.
Schematic illustration of sensing of ALP based on GMP/Eu/DPA (A) and GMP/Tb CPNs (B).
Detection of glyphosate in cereal samples, water samples, soybean and milk samples using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Dual lanthanide-probe based on coordination polymer networks for ratiometric detection of glyphosate in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a new type of coordination polymer networks (CPNs), where both of 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) and guanylate monophosphate (GMP) chelate with Eu3+, are firstly synthesized (GMP/Eu/DPA). After mixing with GMP/Tb CPNs, a novel ratiometric dual lanthanide nanoprobe is constructed. Alkaline phosphohydrolase (ALP) specifically induces the cleavage of phosphate ester group in GMP. Therefore, the addition of ALP causes the fluorescence quenching of GMP/Tb, and then the emissions of GMP/Eu/DPA enhance as the result of the formation of Eu/DPA complexes. Glyphosate, as an organophosphorus pesticide, can vehemently inhibit the catalytic activity of ALP, so a ratiometric detection of glyphosate can be achieved with a linear range from 0.015 to 8.45 μg/mL. The present strategy also shows good recoveries for measuring glyphosate in rice, millet, soybean, milk, tap water, and mountain spring water, suggesting a great potential for applications in foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Coordination polymer networks (CPNs) can be spontaneously self-assembled from nucleotides and lanthanide ions (Aimé, Nishiyabu, Gondo, Kaneko, & Kimizuka, 2008). Especially, the CPNs consisting of guanylate monophosphate (GMP) and terbium (Tb) ions can emit bright luminescence and are useful tool to study the dephosphorylation behavior of phosphatase (Ma et al., 2014). However, except for GMP/Tb, most complexes composed of lanthanides and GMP exhibit weak fluorescence. This is ascribed to enhanced non-radiative quenching effects causing by the vibrionic coupling of the lanthanide excited states with OH oscillators in H2O (Nishiyabu et al., 2009). Nevertheless, when H2O is replaced with other molecules, these lanthanide complexes will be converted to a fluorescent enhanced state. For instance, 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) can bind with europium ions to form chelate complexes, which offer a sharp red emission and maximum luminous intensity in eliminating second-order scattering interference. By virtue of this characteristic, DPA, which is a unique biomarker for bacillus spores, can be measured sensitively (Wang et al., 2017).

Pesticides used to prevent insect pests and regulate plant growth are essential substances for agriculture production (Li et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018). Glyphosate (N-(phosphonmethyl) glycine), as one of the organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), is the world’s most widely produced herbicide (Lee, Zimmerman, Bhullar, & Thurman, 2002), notably for genetically modified plants, which are tolerant to glyphosate (Bonny, 2008; Lee et al., 2017). However, the improper treatment and abuse usually produce glyphosate residue in genetically modified food as well as water, and it may pose a risk to human health (Wei et al., 2013; Werth et al., 2008; Gil, Laguarda-Miro, Camino, & Peris, 2013). The World Health Organization's International Agency for Research on Cancer recently concludes that glyphosate is probably carcinogenic to humans (De Roos et al., 2004; Guyton et al., 2015). Due to the high sensitivity, simplicity, and low cost, the fluorescence-based strategies indicate a great potential in detecting OPs. These methods are usually developed based on the inhibition effects of OPs on the enzyme activity. For example, paraoxon efficiently inhibited the activity of butyryl cholinesterase, which hydrolyzed acetylthiocholine to produce thiocholine; the thiol groups in the products reduced the MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, so the quenching effect of MnO2 on the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were hindered (Yan et al., 2018). Similarly, OPs also prevented the activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and tyrosinase, and then the products changed the fluorescence of Au@Ag nanoparticles (NPs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) (Lv et al., 2016; Yan, Li, Hu, & Su, 2017). In view of these cases, it is found that the detection of OPs is highly dependent on the sensitive response of fluorescent probe to enzyme activity. However, these methods still suffer some limitations, such as high costs of noble metal nanomaterials, complicated reaction process, and poor sensitivity.

Enlightened by the above facts, herein, based on the CPNs involving GMP and dual lanthanide ions, a sensitive sensor for ALP and glyphosate is constructed. At first, a new type of CPNs has been fabricated (called GMP/Eu/DPA), and in these networks the Eu ions are chelated with both DPA and GMP. These CPNs combine the optical activity with binding site for ALP. With the addition of ALP, the phosphate group of GMP ligand is cleaved, so more Eu ions are chelated with DPA and emit strong red emission owning to the antenna effect of DPA (Wang et al., 2017). This single-channel probe shows a wide range of ALP, but the sensitivity is unsatisfactory on account of the low efficiency of Eu3+ (Shuvaev, Starck, & Parker, 2017). Hence, in order to improve the sensitivity, GMP/Tb CPNs are mixed with GMP/Eu/DPA to construct a dual-emission probe of GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA. In this case, with addition of ALP, the fluorescence of GMP/Tb firstly decreases, and then the red emissions of GMP/Eu/DPA enhance. By virtue of the inhibition effect of glyphosate on ALP, the ratiometric detection of glyphosate is first realized. The dual-emission probe shows a good sensitivity to glyphosate and a wide linear range from 0.015 to 8.45 μg/mL can be achieved. Last but not least, the dual-emission probe has been applied for measuring glyphosate in food and water samples with satisfactory results.

Tb (NO3)3, Eu (NO3)3 and Dipicolinic acid (DPA, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid) were purchased from Xiya Reagent Co. Ltd. Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), Guanosine 5′-monophosphate disodium salt hydrate (GMP), Parathion-methyl solution, Methyl viologen dichloride and N-(Phosphonomethyl) glycine (Glyphosate) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), CN-11-3183, Fipronil, Imidacloprid and Acetamiprid were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals are of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), with a quartz cell (1 cm * 1 cm) in the emission mode. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were analyzed using a JEM-2100PLUS (JEOL, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a Cary 300 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra (KBr pellet method) were carried out using a Thermo Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR ESP spectrometer. The pH values of solutions were measured using a pH meter (Mettler Toledo FE20, Switzerland).

Generally, 1 mL GMP disodium salt aqueous solution (5 mM) was added into 1 mL Tb (NO3)3 aqueous solutions (5 mM). The mixture was shaken at room temperature for 30 min and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to obtain a white precipitate. The precipitate was washed three times with deionized water to remove unreacted reactants, and finally the resulting precipitate GMP/Tb was dispersed in 2 mL of deionized water, and then the suspension was stored at 4 °C or lower for further use.

Typically, 500 μL 27 mM GMP aqueous solution was mixed with 500 μL 27 mM Eu (NO3)3 solutions, followed by the addition of 500 μL 22.5 mM DPA solution. After shaking for 100 min, the prepared GMP/Eu/DPA was then allowed to stand at room temperature for further use.

In a typical procedure, Tris-HCl buffer 200 μL (pH 8.4, 250 mM) was added into 10 μL as prepared GMP/Tb solution. Then ALP solution with different concentrations was added into the above solution and diluted with deionized water to 1 mL in a 37 °C water bath. The final concentration of Tris-HCl buffer and GMP/Tb was 50 mM and 25 μM, respectively. After 30 min, 10 μL of GMP/Eu/DPA was added to the mixture, and the reaction was continued in a 37 °C water bath for 30 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength at 270 nm.

Above all, different concentrations of glyphosate (0.015–8.45 μg/mL) and 10 U/L ALP were mixed in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.4, 250 mM) for 30 min. After that, 10 μL GMP/Tb were added to the above solution and react for 30 min. Then, 10 μL of GMP/Eu/DPA was continuously added and reacted for 30 min. All processes are operated at 37 °C in a water bath. Finally, the emission spectra were collected at excitation wavelength of 270 nm.

The rice, millet, soybean and milk are purchased from the local supermarkets directly. According to the reported literature (Wang et al., 2016), the food samples (1 g, rice, millet, soybean or 1 mL milk) were mixed with 20 mL water. Then, 10 mL dichloromethane was added into the mixture and sonicated for 15 min. After centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 10 min, the collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane. Finally, the filtrate was evaporated to 2 mL. Tap water and mountain spring water were obtained from our lab and the mountains around the city. Then these samples were analyzed in accordance with the above-mentioned experiment for glyphosate detection. Then, different concentrations (0.1, 1, 5 μg/mL) of glyphosate standard solutions were added into these six samples and detected by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GMP/Eu, Eu/DPA, GMP/Eu/DPA and GMP/Tb.
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, a lot of reports have studied the features of GMP/Eu and Eu/DPA (Duan et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017). Herein, both of GMP and DPA chelate with Eu3+ to gain the GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs. The differences of the GMP/Eu, Eu/DPA and GMP/Eu/DPA in morphology and optical properties are investigated in detail. In TEM images (Fig. 1 A and 1B), it can be seen clearly that GMP/Eu is nearly spherical with good dispersibility, while Eu/DPA appears as a fairly dense network structure. Although both of Eu/DPA and GMP/Eu exhibit the similar absorption peak around 270 nm in the UV–vis spectra, their luminescence behavior is different (Fig. 1D). The complex of Eu/DPA shows three emission peaks at 592, 615 and 695 nm under the excitation of 270 nm (Fig. 1E), which is consistent with the electronic transitions of Eu3+ excited states from 5D0 to 7FJ (J = 2, 3, respectively) (Gaillard et al., 2013). However, no fluorescence of GMP/Eu can be obvserved due to the quenching effect induced by high-frequency OH oscillators of H2O, and this phenomenon is consistent with the report of Kimizuka’s group (Nishiyabu et al., 2009).

When GMP doped the Eu/DPA complexes, the newly synthesized CPNs display a network structure, but they are more porous compared with Eu/DPA (Fig. 1C). Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectrum of GMP/Eu/DPA is similar to those of Eu/DPA and GMP/Eu, while the red fluorescence of GMP/Eu/DPA is lower than that of Eu/DPA. Furthermore, emissions of the Eu/DPA decrease gradually with increasing the concentration of GMP, and when the concentration of GMP reaches 90 μM, the fluorescence intensity of Eu/DPA is no longer decrease (Fig. S1). These observations indicate that Eu3+ may simultaneously coordinate with GMP and DPA to form nanohybrids. To further identify this hypothesis, the FT-IR spectra of these three nanomaterials have been carried out (Fig. 1F). Comparing the FT-IR spectrum of GMP with that of GMP/Eu, the deformation vibration of PO4 3− in GMP at 1360 cm−1 disappers when GMP integrates with Eu3+, and at the same time a new peak at 1220 cm−1 is present, suggesting that GMP participates in the coordination of Eu3+ by phospholipid bond. For Eu/DPA, the peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibration of CO and CO in ester bond reveal that Eu3+ coordinate with carboxylic acid groups of DPA molecule. For GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs, both of phosphate ester linkage and ester bond exsit in their FT-IR spectrum, and this direct evidence supports that Eu3+ actually coordinates with both GMP and DPA. Moreover, some polar functional groups, such as OH (stretching vibration at 3225 cm−1) and NH2 (stretching vibration at 3345 cm−1) make GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs have good water solubility.

On the other hand, the GMP/Tb is also well prepared. TEM image (Fig. S2A) shows that GMP/Tb has a network structure with nanoscale dimensions. The UV–vis spectrum of GMP/Tb demonstrates the characteristic absorption peaks of Tb3+ at around 270 nm (Fig. S2B). And the four fluorescence peaks at 490, 545, 585 and 620 nm under the 270 nm excitation are ascribed to the energy transfer from the guanine base to the emissive D4 state of the Tb3+ ion (Richardson, 1982; Nishiyabu, Aimé, Gondo, Noguchi, & Kimizuka, 2009).

ALP, an alkaline phosphomonoesterase, specifically induces the cleavage of phosphate ester group (Harris, 1990; Kim, Kim, Choi, & Kim, 2011). Although the chelate complexes Eu/DPA exhibit strong fluorescence, they lack the binding site for ALP. Therefore, the addition of ALP has a negligible influence on the emission of Eu/DPA complexes (Fig. S3 curve a and curve b). While the phosphate ester group of GMP can be hydrolyzed by ALP, it is impossible to achieve the response to ALP due to the non-emissive GMP/Eu CPNs (Fig. S3 curve c and curve d). Herein, the GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs, possessing both of optical activity and binding site for ALP, are a desirable probe. As shown in Fig. S4, because ALP induces the cleavage of GMP/Eu and more Eu/DPA complexes form (Scheme 1 A), the emissions of GMP/Eu/DPA enhance gradually with increasing the amounts of ALP. The fluorescence intensity at 615 nm displays a linear relationship with the concentration of ALP from 10 to 200 U/L and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 8 U/L based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3:1. Comparing with other methods for ALP detection, the GMP/Eu/DPA probe shows a wide linear range of ALP, but the sensitivity is unsatisfactory (Table S1).

Besides, GMP/Tb CPNs emit bright green luminescence and can also be used to study the dephosphorylation behavior of phosphatase. In the presence of ALP, the phosphate ester group of GMP is hydrolyzed and GMP/Tb is dissociated (Scheme 1B). As a result, the energy transfer between GMP and Tb3+ reduces, leading to the fluorescence quenching (Fu & Turro, 1999). It can be seen that the luminescence at 490 nm is sensitive to the concentration of ALP, and the linear range of ALP ranges from 0.5 to 15 U/L with LOD of 0.3 U/L (Fig. S5). The outcome proves that the GMP/Tb CPNs reveal a good sensitivity for ALP, but the linearity range is relatively narrow compared with other methods in Table S1.

As mentioned above, a dual lanthanide-probe (GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA) may be an ideal candidate for ALP sensing with a good sensitivity as well as a wide linear range. In Fig. S6, the GMP/Tb and GMP/Eu/DPA all exhibit similar UV–vis absorption peaks around 270 nm, indicating that the maximum emission efficiency of the mixed probes can be obtained for the same excitation wavelength at 270 nm; the dual lanthanide-probe shows five emission peaks, which are consistent with the electronic transitions of Tb3+ (490, 545 nm) and Eu3+ (590, 615, 695 nm), respectively. To further investigate the chemical stability of the mixed probes, the influences of time, ionic strength and pH values on the emissions of GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA are explored. As depicted in Fig. S7, the GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA can maintain stable emissions even after one month; both the ionic strength (NaCl from 0.1 to 120 mM) and pH values (from 1 to 10) also introduce a negligible effect on this dual lanthanide-probe.

The GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA probe can be obtained by two ways: one is inclusion of GMP/Eu/DPA by GMP/Tb; in this way, the GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs are synthesized at first and then adding GMP and Tb ions to form a GMP/Tb shell capped on GMP/Eu/DPA in situ. However, due to the loose and porous structure features of CPNs, the GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs can not be included completely by the GMP/Tb shell. Therefore, the addition of ALP can simultaneously cleave the phospholipid bonds of GMP in both GMP/Tb and GMP/Eu/DPA, leading to the simultaneous coordination of DPA with Tb3+ and Eu3+ (Fig. S8). Because DPA can act as a sensitizer, both Tb/DPA and Eu/DPA exhibit greater fluorescence (De Sa et al., 2000), and this way is invalid for ratiometric detection of ALP.

Another one is physical mixing of GMP/Tb with GMP/Eu/DPA, and the addition order plays an important role in this process. Order 1 (Fig. S9): when the GMP/Tb and GMP/Eu/DPA mixed together at first, the addition of ALP increases all emissions of the dual lanthanide-probe. The reason is also that the ALP hydrolyzes the phosphate ester groups in both of GMP/Tb and GMP/Eu/DPA, and then DPA chelates with both Tb and Eu ions. Order 2: the GMP/Tb firstly reacts with ALP and then GMP/Eu/DPA is introduced. In this process, when the concentration of ALP is relative low (0.1–10 U/L), the phosphate groups from GMP/Tb are primarily cleaved, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of GMP/Tb, while the emissions of GMP/Eu/DPA have almost no change (Fig. 2 A). However, with further rising the level of ALP (from 10 to 300 U/L), the fluoresence of GMP/Tb changes mildly, but the emssions of GMP/Eu/DPA ehance obviously (Fig. 2B). Due to the complete dissociation of GMP/Tb, the redundant ALP hydrolyzes the phosphate ester groups in GMP/Eu/DPA, giving rise to the formation of Eu/DPA complexes, which emit strong emission.

The experimental conditions on the response to ALP, including pH and reaction time, are optimized. With the addition of 10 U/L ALP, it can be seen that the quenching efficiency enhances with increasing pH; when the pH value is higher than 8.4, the activity of ALP is greatly elevated, so pH 8.4 is considered as the optimized pH value (Figs.S10A and B). This result is also consistent with the reported works (Stoop et al., 2011; Kulandaivel, S. et al., 2015). Additionally, as the reaction time is longer than 30 min, the fluorescence of GMP/Tb gradually reduces to a constant level in the presence of 10 U/L ALP, while there are few changes in the emissions of GMP/Eu/DPA (Fig. S10C and D). The result indicates that ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis of the nucleotide's phosphate ester group is completed within 30 min. Besides, as well known, the highest activity of enzyme is usually obtained at 37 °C (Gao, Deng, Wang, Miró, & Tang, 2014; Tang et al., 2016).

Under the optimum conditions, the ratiometric detection of ALP can be realized, and linear range of ALP is from 0.1 to 300 U/L with LOD 0.08 U/L (Fig. 2C and D). By comparison, this linear range is wider and the detection limit is lower (Table S1). Hence, this strategy is superior due to the combination of high sensitivity attributed to the GMP/Tb CPNs and a wide linearity range ascribed to GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs.

To evaluate the selectivity toward ALP, a series of metal ions and amino acids as well as proteins/enzymes are tested as controls, including K+, Na+, Mg2+, leucine (Leu), Isoleucine (Ile), Phenylalanine (Phe), Methionine (Met), Arginine (Arg), Lysine (Lys), Threonine (Thr), Histidine (His), Glycine (Gly), Bovine albumin (BSA), Glucose Oxidase (GOD), Trypsin, Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP), Lysozyme (LZM), Glutathione (GSH). Herein, the concentration of ALP is selected about 10 U/L, which quenches the fluorescence of GMP/Tb, while GMP/Eu/DPA CPNs maintain constant emissions, so the fluorescence ratio of F490/F615 reduces obviously. However, no conspicuous change in F490/F615 is observed after adding other interfering substances (Fig. 3 ). Especially, the detection of ALP is not interfered in the presence of these interferences. The good selectivity is ascribed to specific hydrolysis of phosphate groups by ALP, indicating that the dual lanthanide-probe is a promising candidate for ALP measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of Ops, glyphosate can efficiently inhibit the activity of ALP (Ayyagari et al., 1995), so the fluorescent-sensing platform (GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA-ALP) is employed to detect glyphosate. The ALP concentration may play an important role in sensitive response to glyphosate. When the concentration of ALP is 10 U/L, the dual lanthanide-probe shows the sensitive detection of glyphosate (Fig. S11). As depicted in Fig. 4 A, in the presence of ALP (10 U/L), the fluorescence intensity of GMP/Tb quenches completely and the GMP/Eu/DPA can sustain the original level; with increasing the concentration of glyphosate from 0.015 to 8.45 μg/mL, the emissions of GMP/Tb gradully recover and the fluorescence of GMP/Eu/DPA has almost no change, leading to the linear response of F490/F615 (Fig. S5B). The LOD for glyphosate is about 0.007 μg/mL. By comparison with other methods (Table S2), although our strategy requires more time, the synthesis of GMP/Tb@GMP/Eu/DPA GPNs is simple and time-saving. What is more, the ratiometric detection provides a build-in correction for the environmental effects and facilitates quantification in intricate objects and this method displays a good sensitivity and a wide linear range of glyphosate.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method for glyphosate in food and water samples, some common coexistence substances are investigated, including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Leu, Ile, Phe, Met, Arg, Lys, Thr, His and Gly. As shown in Fig. 4C, although the concentrations of these substances are 100 times higher than that of glyphosate, only glyphosate remarkably increases the ratio of F490/F615. Furthermore, the accurate detection of glyphosate can also be achieved in the presence of these interferences. To further illustrate the specificity of the proposed system for glyphosate detection, five common pesticides: fipronil, imidacloprid, acetamiprid, methyl-viologen, and parathion-methyl are investigated (Fig. 4D). Other pesticides do not influence on the detection of glyphosate due to the absence of phosphate ester group in their structures (Fig. S12), indicating that the proposed probe shows a high selectivity toward glyphosate.

As important parameters to assess a strategy in practical application, the precision and reproducibility of the proposed strategy are investigated. The intraday and interday precisions of the assay are determined by estimating the corresponding response 3 times on the same day and on 3 different days over a period of one week (Table S3). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the intraday precision (CV%) is in the range from 1.3 to 2.2%, and that of interday (CV%) is about 1.0–6.2%, suggesting that this method shows a good stability and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed method is further assessed through examining glyphosate in rice, millet, soybean, milk, tap water, and mountain spring water. There is no glyphosate found in these samples. Then, three different concentrations (0.1, 1, 5 μg/mL) of glyphosate are spiked. As listed in Table 1 , the recoveries of glyphosate in these spiked samples vary from 96 to 105% and the RSD of measure precision (CV%) is in the range from 0.5 to 2.7%, revealing that the detection of glyphosate based on the proposed method is reliable and extremely accurate.

In addition, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) recent set Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) of glyphosate ranging from 25 to 2.0 × 103 μg/kg depending on the type of food (European Food Safety Authority, 2015). The liner range of glyphosate in this work can be calculated as 15–8.45 × 103 μg/Kg (1 g sample in 1 mL test solution). This linear range is wider than the range of glyphosate MRLs provided by EFSA. Thus, this strategy possesses great potential for applications in food and water samples.

On a basis of the finding that ALP specifically induces the cleavage of phosphate ester group in GMP, a ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor which is mixed with GMP/Tb and GMP/Eu/DPA is constructed. This newly dual lanthanide-probe not only takes full advantage of the optical property of mentioned two lanthanide ions, but also combines the ultrahigh sensitivity with a wide linear range of ALP. With the addition of glyphosate, the activity of ALP is inhibited, so the probe is successfully applied to measuring glyphosate in food and water samples with good recoveries. Therefore, this dual lanthanide-probe is an ideal analytical tool in the detection of glyphosate in foods.
Fei Qu and Han Wang contributed equally to this work.
Fei Qu: Writing- Reviewing. Han Wang: Experiment, Writing- Original draft preparation. Jinmao You: Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Youth Fund Project) (21405093), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2019QB010), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University, China (BSQD20130117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126815.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
